items.title: Anti-M2: key serological marker
items.contentHtml:
Anti-M2

  • Anti-M2 (E2 subunit of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex) is the most specific marker (>95%) for primary biliary cirrhosis.
  • Present in ~90-95% of patients; Its absence requires searching for other autoantibodies (anti-gp210, anti-sp100) to confirm the diagnosis.
  • Titer >1:40 is considered positive; It does not correlate with histological severity or response to treatment.
  • Trap: It can appear in autoimmune hepatitis (rare) or in overlap syndromes; always interpret with biochemical cholestasis.

items.title: Biochemical cholestasis pattern
items.contentHtml:
Biochemical cholestasis

  • Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is the most sensitive enzyme; its elevation >1.5 times the upper normal limit is a diagnostic criterion along with positive Anti-M2.
  • The GGT rises in parallel; helps differentiate cholestasis of bone origin (elevated FA with normal GGT).
  • Bilirubin and bile acids are elevated in advanced phases; Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia >2 mg/dL predicts worse prognosis (MELD and Mayo Clinic score).
  • Trap: atrial fibrillation can fluctuate; Repeat every 3 months to confirm persistent pattern.

items.title: Associated autoimmune diseases
items.contentHtml:
Autoimmune associations

  • Up to 30-50% of patients with primary biliary cirrhosis present another autoimmune disease: Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Sjögren's syndrome, limited scleroderma (CREST), rheumatoid arthritis or celiac disease.
  • CREST syndrome (calcinosis, Raynaud, sclerodactyly, telangiectasias) is the most classic association with primary biliary cirrhosis.
  • Trap: the presence of anti-RO/anti-LA autoantibodies suggests secondary Sjögren, not lupus.

items.foco: Pruritus as a common initial symptom
items.title: Cholestatic pruritus: initial management
items.contentHtml:
Itching

  • Itching is the initial symptom in ~50% of cases; may precede jaundice by years.
  • Mechanism: accumulation of bile acids and lysophosphatidate (autotaxin); does not correlate with bilirubin levels.
  • First line: cholestyramine 4 g/day (max 16 g/day), separated by 2-4 hours from other drugs to avoid interference.
  • Alternatives: rifampicin 150-300 mg/day, opioid antagonists (naltrexone), or serotonin reuptake inhibitors (sertraline).
  • Trap: the itching worsens with heat and only partially improves with antihistamines.

items.foco: Chronic fatigue and its impact on quality of life
items.title: Fatigue: evaluation and management
items.contentHtml:
Chronic fatigue

  • Chronic fatigue affects 50-80% of patients; It is the symptom that most impacts quality of life, regardless of histological severity.
  • There is no specific approved pharmacological treatment; Regular physical exercise, cognitive-behavioral therapy and ruling out hypothyroidism or anemia are recommended.
  • Modafinil has shown benefit in small trials, but is not routinely used.
  • Trap: fatigue does not correlate with the biochemical response to ursodeoxycholic acid.

items.title: AUD: first line therapy
items.contentHtml:
Ursodeoxycholic acid

  • Dose: 13-15 mg/kg/day in a single or divided dose; It is the only drug approved as a first line.
  • It reduces histological progression and delays the need for transplant; does not reverse established fibrosis.
  • Optimal response defined by Paris-I criteria (atrial fibrillation <3× ULN and AST <2× ULN and normal bilirubin after 1 year) or Barcelona criteria (decrease in ALP >40% or normalization at 12 months).
  • Trap: in patients with incomplete response, consider adding fibrates (bezafibrate) or obeticholic acid as a second line.

items.foco: Risk of portal hypertension and cirrhosis
items.title: Progression to portal hypertension
items.contentHtml:
Portal hypertension

  • Primary biliary cirrhosis progresses to cirrhosis in 10-20 years without treatment; portal hypertension can appear even in pre-cirrhotic stages (portal fibrosis without nodules).
  • The portal pressure gradient >10 mmHg defines clinically significant portal hypertension; screening with endoscopy every 1-2 years for esophageal varices.
  • The Mayo Clinic score (based on age, bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time, and edema) predicts survival and risk of decompensation.
  • Trap: ascites and encephalopathy are late events; Portal hypertension may precede jaundice.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: CBP vs. CEP
items.contentHtml:
Primary sclerosing cholangitis

  • Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) presents with cholestasis but Anti-M2 negative and cholangiography pattern with multifocal “rosary” strictures.
  • Primary biliary cirrhosis has Anti-M2 positive and biopsy with granulomas; PSC is associated with inflammatory bowel disease in 60-80% of cases.
  • In PSC the alkaline phosphatase is fluctuating; cholangioresonance is the diagnostic technique of choice.
  • Trap: both can cause pruritus and fatigue; The absence of Anti-M2 requires PSC to be ruled out by cholangiography.

items.foco: Finding of granulomas in liver biopsy
items.title: Non-caseating granulomas: histological hallmark
items.contentHtml:
Hepatic granulomas

  • Liver biopsy shows non-caseating granulomas in stage I (portal) in ~50% of patients; They are aggregates of epithelioid histiocytes with lymphocytes.
  • Histological stage (Ludwig): I (portal), II (periportal), III (fibrous septa), intravenous (cirrhosis). The presence of granulomas suggests early diagnosis.
  • The biopsy is not mandatory if there is a positive Anti-M2, high FA and compatible symptoms; it is reserved for atypical cases or suspected overlap.
  • Trap: granulomas also appear in sarcoidosis, infections (TB) or hepatitis due to drugs; The serological and clinical context is key.

items.foco: Deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins due to cholestasis
items.title: Malabsorption of vitamins A, D, E, K
items.contentHtml:
Deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins

  • Chronic cholestasis reduces the secretion of bile acids to the intestine, causing malabsorption of vitamins A, D, E and K.
  • Deficiency of vitamin D is the most common (osteopenia/osteoporosis); It is recommended to monitor levels and supplement with vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day plus calcium.
  • Deficiency of vitamin K prolongs prothrombin time; It is corrected with phytomenadione 10 mg/day orally or parenterally.
  • Trap: supplementation should be started early, even before symptoms appear.

items.title: Epidemiology: typical profile
items.contentHtml:
Epidemiology

  • Primary biliary cirrhosis predominantly affects middle-aged women (40-60 years), with a female:male ratio of 9:1.
  • The incidence is higher in Nordic and Anglo-Saxon countries; In Latin America the prevalence is lower but increasing due to better diagnosis.
  • Genetic factors: HLA-DR8, DR3, DR2; environmental factors: recurrent urinary infections, smoking, exposure to toxins.
  • Trap: in men the disease is usually more aggressive and with a worse response to ursodeoxycholic acid.

items.foco: Indication for liver transplant in advanced stages
items.title: Liver transplant: criteria and prognosis
items.contentHtml:
Liver transplant

  • Indications: bilirubin >6 mg/dL, MELD >15, refractory ascites, encephalopathy, intractable pruritus or disabling fatigue.
  • Post-transplant survival is excellent (>85% at 5 years), but recurrence of biliary cirrhosis Primary occurs in 20-30% at 10 years (detected by persistent Anti-M2 and biopsy).
  • The Mayo Clinic score (pre-transplant risk model) helps prioritize; a value >7.8 suggests high mortality without transplant.
  • Trap: intractable pruritus is an accepted indication even without established cirrhosis.
